episode_title: Audio long read: Many people have no mental imagery. What’s going on in their brains?
summary: This episode explores aphantasia, a neurological condition affecting approximately 4% of the population characterized by the absence or severe weakness of mental imagery. The episode traces the scientific discovery and study of aphantasia, beginning with neurologist Adam Zeman's 2015 paper that coined the term, and examines recent neuroscience research investigating how brains of people with aphantasia differ in visual cortex activity and inter-regional connectivity. Researchers have identified that aphantasia involves both an inability to consciously experience mental images and difficulties voluntarily generating them, despite the presence of visual representations in the brain. The condition appears to have genetic components, may be more common in technical professions, and notably reduces the vividness of autobiographical memories while having minimal impact on most behavioral and cognitive tasks.
science_topics: Visual cortex activity and mental imagery generation
Binocular rivalry as a measurement technique for mental imagery strength
Brain connectivity between visual and frontal regions
Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) studies of imagery
Machine learning algorithms for decoding brain activity
Hallucinations in schizophrenia and Parkinson's disease
Synesthesia and prosopagnosia (face blindness) as comparative neurological phenomena
claims_to_note: Aphantasia affects approximately 4% of the population
Aphantasia has a genetic component with tenfold increased likelihood if a sibling has aphantasia
Aphantasia may be more common in people in scientific and technical professions than in arts careers
People with aphantasia perform relatively well on standard memory assessments and mental rotation tasks
Visual cortex shows activity during imagery attempts in people with aphantasia similar to control individuals
Aphantasia reduces the richness and vividness of autobiographical memories
People with aphantasia can recognize faces and identify spatial locations as well as those with typical imagery
Over 20,000 people have contacted Adam Zeman about aphantasia since 2015 New York Times article
key_quotes: When I close my eyes, there's absolutely nothing there, Shine recalls telling his colleagues.

The fact that there are people with a complete lack of mental imagery brings fresh ways to study this internal experience... how does it affect our emotion, our perception, our attention, our memory? We can understand this with aphantasia.
The way that the world shows up to people has a massive massive variation to it... accounting for this variation is central to understanding how human brains work.

Ending Discrimination Against People with Mental and Substance Use Disorders: The Evidence for Stigma Change
publications.description: Estimates indicate that as many as 1 in 4 Americans will experience a mental health problem or will misuse alcohol or drugs in their lifetimes. These disorders are among the most highly stigmatized health conditions in the United States, and they remain barriers to full participation in society in areas as basic as education, housing, and employment. Improving the lives of people with mental health and substance abuse disorders has been a priority in the United States for more than 50 years. The Community Mental Health Act of 1963 is considered a major turning point in America's efforts to improve behavioral healthcare. It ushered in an era of optimism and hope and laid the groundwork for the consumer movement and new models of recovery. The consumer movement gave voice to people with mental and substance use disorders and brought their perspectives and experience into national discussions about mental health. publications.title: Final Review of the Study on Supplemental Treatment Approaches of Low-Activity Waste at the Hanford Nuclear Reservation: Review #4
publications.description: The U.S. Department of Energy's Office of Environmental Management is responsible for managing and cleaning up the waste and contamination at the Hanford Nuclear Reservation, the nation's biggest and most complex nuclear cleanup challenge. At the site, 177 underground tanks collectively contain about 211 million liters of waste that includes high-activity and low-activity materials. At the request of Congress, Final Review of the Study on Supplemental Treatment Approaches of Low-Activity Waste at the Hanford Nuclear Reservation: Review #4 focuses on approaches for treatment and disposal of the supplemental portion of the low-activity waste from the tanks. This review report discusses developments since the publication of Review #3 and provides a summary of public comments on the third committee review report. The authoring committee then shares their views on these comments and whether they change any of the findings or recommendations in the third review report.

Risk Assessment Techniques for Transportation Asset Management: Conduct of Research
publications.description: "The assessment of climatic and extreme weather risks is increasingly becoming important to the operation of transportation agencies. There are easy-to-use tools and techniques that can be implemented by agencies. [This report] discusses how to assess risks and summarizes 12 studies that demonstrate how to enhance the measurement of risks, quantify risks, and better link risk management processes with the appropriate tools."--Publisher's website

Electricity Use in Rural and Islanded Communities: Summary of a Workshop
publications.description: On behalf of the Quadrennial Energy Review (QER) Task Force, the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine hosted a workshop on February 8-9, 2016, titled "Electricity Use in Rural and Islanded Communities." The objective of the workshop was to help the QER Task Force public outreach efforts by focusing on communities with unique electricity challenges. The workshop explored challenges and opportunities for reducing electricity use and associated greenhouse gas emissions while improving electricity system reliability and resilience in rural and islanded communities. This report summarizes the presentation and discussion of the workshop.
publications.committee: Board on Energy and Environmental Systems

publications.title: Improving Representation in Clinical Trials and Research: Building Research Equity for Women and Underrepresented Groups
publications.description: The United States has long made substantial investments in clinical research with the goal of improving the health and well-being of our nation. There is no doubt that these investments have contributed significantly to treating and preventing disease and extending human life. Nevertheless, clinical research faces a critical shortcoming. Currently, large swaths of the U.S. population, and those that often face the greatest health challenges, are less able to benefit from these discoveries because they are not adequately represented in clinical research studies. While progress has been made with representation of white women in clinical trials and clinical research, there has been little progress in the last three decades to increase participation of racial and ethnic minority population groups. This underrepresentation is compounding health disparities, with serious consequences for underrepresented groups and for the nation. At the request of Congress, Improving Representation in Clin
publications.committee: Committee on Women in Science Engineering and Medicine
